name: Marismas de Santoña, Escalante, San Mamés de Meruela – circular from Santoña
name_original: Marismas de Santoña, Escalante, San Mamés de Meruela – circular desde Santoña
region_intro: Road cycling around Santoña offers diverse landscapes, from coastal paths to inland hills and extensive marshlands. The region is characterized by its unique position, surrounded by the Santoña, Victoria and Joyel Marshes Natural Park, prominent hills like Buciero, and the Cantabrian Sea. This varied terrain provides a range of road cycling experiences, including routes with significant elevation gains and more relaxed, flatter sections. Best road cycling routes around Santoña The most popular road cycling route is Santoña Marshes and Collado de Asón Loop, a 63.6 miles (102.4 km) trail that takes 5 hours 33 minutes to complete. This challenging route features significant elevation gain and diverse scenery. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Marismas de Santoña, Escalante, San Mamés de Meruela – circular from Santoña, an easy 15.3 miles (24.6 km) path. This route offers a relaxed experience through the marshlands with less elevation. Local road cyclists also love the Santoña Marshes – Marshes Viewpoint loop from Santoña, a 27.3 miles (43.9 km) trail leading through marshlands and offering scenic viewpoints, often completed in about 1 hour 52 minutes. Road cycling around Santoña is defined by marshlands, coastal views, and hills, offering a blend of flat and undulating terrain. The network provides options for different ability levels, from easy loops to more challenging climbs. The routes in Santoña are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 70 reviews. More than 6000 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Santoña's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of road cycling terrain can I expect in Santoña?
A: Santoña offers a diverse range of road cycling terrain. You'll find routes that combine coastal views with inland exploration, passing through extensive marshlands, rolling hills, and along the Cantabrian Sea. The area provides a blend of flat sections, particularly through the marshlands, and more challenging undulating terrain with significant elevation gains in the hilly areas. Most surfaces are well-paved, though some more demanding segments might include unpaved sections.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners or those looking for an easy ride?
A: Yes, Santoña has options for all skill levels. For an easier, more relaxed experience, consider routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1012734595/marismas-de-santona-escalante-san-mames-de-meruela-circular-from-santona" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Marismas de Santoña, Escalante, San Mamés de Meruela – circular from Santoña&lt;/a&gt;. This 24.6 km route offers less elevation and takes you through the scenic marshlands. There are 16 easy routes available in the region, providing gentle rides.

Q: What are some challenging road cycling routes around Santoña for experienced cyclists?
A: Experienced cyclists seeking a challenge will find plenty of demanding routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1005118280/santona-marshes-and-collado-de-ason-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santoña Marshes and Collado de Asón Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a notable option, covering over 102 km with significant elevation gain (around 1,920m). This route offers diverse scenery and demanding climbs. Overall, there are 33 difficult routes in the area for those looking for a strenuous ride.

Q: When is the best time of year to go road cycling in Santoña?
A: Santoña is an excellent destination for outdoor activities, including road cycling, throughout much of the year. The region benefits from a mild climate. However, for the most comfortable conditions, spring and early autumn generally offer pleasant temperatures and less extreme weather, making them ideal for exploring the diverse landscapes.

Q: What natural attractions or landmarks can I explore near the cycling routes?
A: The Santoña area is rich in natural beauty and historical sites. You can explore the ecological wetlands of the &lt;a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Santo%C3%B1a,_Victoria_and_Joyel_Marshes_Natural_Park" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santoña, Victoria and Joyel Marshes Natural Park&lt;/a&gt;, which is a crucial winter refuge for migratory birds. Other notable features include Buciero Hill, offering panoramic views and access to sites like San Martín Fort and the Horse Lighthouse. Along the routes, you might encounter highlights such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1834895" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Devil's Eye&lt;/a&gt;, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4545693" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Ojerada Caves&lt;/a&gt;, or the scenic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4136054" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Piedra Pombera Beach&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any family-friendly road cycling routes in Santoña?
A: Yes, Santoña offers several family-friendly road cycling options, particularly those that are classified as easy. Routes through the Marismas de Santoña, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1012734595/marismas-de-santona-escalante-san-mames-de-meruela-circular-from-santona" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Marismas de Santoña, Escalante, San Mamés de Meruela – circular from Santoña&lt;/a&gt;, are generally flatter and provide a relaxed experience suitable for families. There are 16 easy routes available, many of which are well-suited for a family outing.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Santoña?
A: The road cycling routes in Santoña are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 70 reviews. Cyclists often praise the diverse scenery, which combines coastal views with marshlands and hills, and the variety of routes catering to different skill levels. The unique natural environment of the Santoña, Victoria and Joyel Marshes Natural Park is also a frequently highlighted feature.

Q: Are there places to stop for refreshments or cafes along the road cycling routes?
A: Many road cycling routes in Santoña pass through or near towns and villages where you can find cafes, restaurants, and other establishments for refreshments. The region is known for its local charm, so you'll likely encounter opportunities to refuel and experience local cuisine, especially in Santoña itself and other coastal towns like Laredo or Escalante.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available in Santoña?
A: There are over 100 road cycling routes available around Santoña, offering a wide variety of distances and difficulty levels. This includes 16 easy routes, 51 moderate routes, and 33 difficult routes, ensuring there's something for every cyclist.

Q: Are there any unique experiences, like boat crossings, on the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, some routes in the Santoña area offer unique experiences. For instance, certain longer journeys or loops might incorporate a boat crossing between Santoña and Laredo, providing a distinctive way to connect different parts of your ride and enjoy the coastal scenery from a different perspective.

Q: What are the parking options for cyclists in Santoña?
A: Santoña, being a popular destination, offers various parking options. You can typically find public parking areas within the town. For specific routes, it's advisable to check the starting points, as some may have designated parking or be easily accessible from central Santoña where parking is available. Public transport connections might also be an option for reaching certain starting points.
tips: This natural park is the most important wetland on the Cantabrian coast. Its 6,679 hectares are full of unique marine fauna and are home to more than 121 species of waterfowl. The tides constantly change this landscape that can be seen from the different shores and from the bridge that crosses it.
The 'old fountain' and typical old laundry.

At the entrance roundabout to Castillo Siete Villas , a town in the Cantabrian municipality of Arnuero, this tribute to the pilot and neighbor Salvador Hedilla was inaugurated on July 2, 2016, coinciding with the centenary of the first flight he made from the Peninsula to the Balearic Islands, specifically from Barcelona to Palma de Mallorca. For this flight he used a monoplane designed by him and built under his direction, whose replica made by inmates of El Dueso Prison from a photograph and with materials provided by the city council can be seen in the roundabout. His feat earned him the Mediterranean Cup, a gold trophy donated by the King.
